Jeffrey Dahmer, also known as the "Milwaukee Cannibal" or "Milwaukee Monster," was an American serial killer and sex offender. He was born on May 21, 1960, and died on November 28, 1994.

Jeffrey Dahmer is known for committing a series of heinous and gruesome crimes, including the murder and dismemberment of 17 men and boys between 1978 and 1991. His victims were mostly young men of color, and he would often target them in gay bars, parks, or other public places.

Dahmer's methods were particularly disturbing, as he would lure his victims to his apartment, where he would drug and kill them. He would then engage in necrophilia and cannibalism, and even preserve some of their body parts, such as skulls and bones, in his apartment.

Dahmer was arrested on July 22, 1991, after one of his intended victims, Tracy Edwards, escaped from his apartment and led the police to the scene. Dahmer was subsequently charged with multiple counts of murder, and he was sentenced to 15 consecutive life terms in prison.

Dahmer was killed in prison on November 28, 1994, by fellow inmate Osvaldo Durruthy, who bludgeoned him to death with a 20-inch metal weight.

It's worth noting that Dahmer's crimes were widely publicized and shocked the nation, leading to a renewed focus on the need for stricter laws and better mental health services to prevent such atrocities from occurring in the future.
